text: A 67-year-old gentleman with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and history of diffuse pulmonary interstitial disease is being prepared for diagnostic thoracoscopy.
The patient is placed in the standard posterior lateral thoracotomy position to allow immediate open access should this become necessary.
An iodine impregnated drape is used.
The patient's shoulder is on the right, his hip to the left, and his right nipple is inferior and to the right.
Three incisions are necessary to perform the procedure.
The middle incision through which the video thoracoscope is placed is made in the mid axillary line in the sixth intercostal space.
The anterior and posterior incisions are made in the fifth intercostal space.
A 12 millimeter trocar with protective shield in a deactivated position is inserted through the mid axillary line incision and directed over the top of the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.
The stylet is then removed and the video thoracoscope is placed through the trocar.
The entire right hemithorax is explored at this point.
Inspection of the thoracic cavity is facilitated by use of a double lumen endotracheal tube, which allows single lung ventilation.
The anterolateral aspect of the right upper lobe and adjacent chest wall are well visualized and show diffuse anthracosis and intrapleural lipoma.
The video camera now shows some adhesions between the visceral and parietal pleura, which have been taken down.
Note the clarity of the intercostal bundles.
The lower lobe is markedly inflamed and appears edematous.
The trocar is secured to the skin with a 2-0 silk suture.
A second 12-millimeter trocar is inserted through the anterior axillary line in the fifth intercostal space.
The incision is enlarged with a hemostat.
Blunt finger dissection is employed to free any underlying adhesions.
The trocar can now be safely placed under direct thoracoscopic visualization.
The thoracoscope is redirected toward the posterior axillary line prior to insertion of the third and final trocar.
This is positioned in the fifth intercostal space.
Again this is safely accomplished under direct visualization.
With all three trocars positioned to allow for a triangulation configuration, we are now ready to proceed with the lung biopsy.
A diseased area of the upper lobe is identified with the viewing thoracoscope.
A grasper is passed through the posteriorly placed trocar to secure a segment of lung parenchyma.
An endo stapler is inserted through the anterior trocar and positioned across this segment.
an attempt should be made to include the entire biopsy specimen with the stapler.
When the stapler is fixed, six rows of staples are placed and a blade automatically cuts between rows three and four of staples.
Endoclips and endocisors can be used to divide small amounts of remaining lung tissue in lieu of reloading the endostapler.
The specimen is then slowly withdrawn through the trocar and sent for permanent sectioning.
The trocars are redirected in order to obtain a second biopsy from the lower lobe.
Once again, the grasper provides tension so that the endo stapler can be positioned across a flat surface.
Endoclips and scissors complete the division of the specimen as before.
Note that the endoclips stay on the lung parenchyma, remaining in the chest.
If the specimen is too large to pass through the trocar, the grasper with specimen and trocar itself are removed simultaneously.
A suction probe allows identification of any significant bleeding at the biopsy site.
Irrigation, cauterization, and further endoclip application may be employed at this time if necessary.
After reinspection of all biopsy sites for hemostasis and air leaks has been completed, a 32 French chest tube is inserted through the anterior trocar and directed anteriorly and apically.
This allows complete expansion of the lung.
All trocars are removed under direct visualization and the incisions are closed.
The thoracoscopic lung biopsy is tolerated well by the patient.
Chest tube drainage is minimal.
Air leaks, if present, resolve in 24 to 36 hours.
